en.extract:
The fiery minivet (Pericrocotus igneus) is a species of bird in the family Campephagidae. Its range includes Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia and the Philippines. Its natural habitats are broadleaf, secondary and coastal forests. It is threatened by forest clearance and has been assessed as near-threatened by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN).


Taxonomy
This species was described from Malacca by Edward Blyth in 1846. The species name is from the Latin igneus "fiery". Harry C. Oberholser described the larger subspecies Pericrocotus igneus trophis from Simeulue in 1912. A 2010 molecular phylogenetic study confirmed the previously hypothesised relationship that the fiery minivet and small minivet (Pericrocotus cinnamomeus) are sister species.


Description
The fiery minivet is 15–16.5 cm (5.9–6.5 in) long. It is sexually dimorphic. The male has a glossy black head and mantle and an orange-red back. The wings are mostly glossy black, with orange-red edges to the secondary coverts and an orange-red patch on the flight feathers. The gradated tail is black and orange-red. The chin and throat are glossy black, and the rest of the underparts is orange-red. The eyes are dark brown, and the beak and feet are black. The female has a grey head, with orange lores and eye-rims. The back is lead grey, and the rump is orange-red. The wings are darker grey, and the wing-patch is paler than the male's. The underparts are yellow. The juvenile bird has sooty brown upperparts and sooty black flight feathers. The underparts are off-white from the chin to upper belly, the rest being pale yellow. After a post-juvenile moult, it becomes similar to the adult female.


Distribution and habitat
This species is found in Tenasserim, the Thai-Malay Peninsula, Sumatra and its satellite islands, Borneo and Palawan. It is locally extinct in Singapore. It lives in the canopy of lowland broadleaf forest, forest edges, peat swamp forest, well-grown secondary forest, and coastal and mangrove forest. It is found up to 1,200 m (3,900 ft) in elevation, mainly below 600 m (2,000 ft). It sometimes visits wooded gardens near forests.


Behaviour
This minivet occurs in groups and also joins mixed-species foraging flocks. Its contact call is a swee-eet or twee-eet. It catches insects in the air and from leaves and branches. Breeding has been observed from May to July. The shallow cup nest is built on a fork of a tree from twigs and fibres and camouflaged with lichen and pieces of bark. The eggs are pale yellowish, with brown and grey marks. Moulting has been recorded from June to September.


Status
Deforestation due to logging and land conversion has likely caused the population to decline at a moderate speed. Forest fires are also a threat, such as in 1997 and 1998. The IUCN has assessed it as a near-threatened species.


References


External links
Image at ADW
 Media related to Pericrocotus igneus at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Pericrocotus igneus at Wikispecies

The '''fiery minivet''' ('''''Pericrocotus igneus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Campephagidae]]. Its range includes Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia and the Philippines. Its natural [[habitat]]s are broadleaf, secondary and coastal forests. It is threatened by forest clearance and has been assessed as near-threatened by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN).

==Taxonomy==
This species was described from [[Malacca]] by [[Edward Blyth]] in 1846.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Blyth|first1=Edward|title=Notices and Descriptions of various New or Little Known Species of Birds|journal=Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal|date=1846|volume=15|pages=309–310|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40122850}}</ref> The species name is from the [[Latin]] ''igneus'' "fiery".<ref>{{cite book|last1=Jobling|first1=James A.|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names|date=2010|publisher=Bloomsbury|isbn=978-1-4081-3326-2|page=202|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-RfSBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA202}}</ref> [[Harry C. Oberholser]] described the larger subspecies ''Pericrocotus igneus trophis'' from [[Simeulue]] in 1912.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Oberholser|first1=Harry C.|title=Descriptions of one hundred and four new species and sub-species from the Barussan Islands and Sumatra|journal=Smithsonian Miscellaneous Collections|date=1912|volume=60|issue=7|page=14|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8910444}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|editor1-last=Gill|editor1-first=F.|editor2-last=Donsker|editor2-first=D.|title=Bristlehead, butcherbirds, woodswallows, ioras, cuckooshrikes|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/|website=IOC World Bird List Version 8.1|access-date=18 May 2018}}</ref> A 2010 [[molecular phylogenetic]] study confirmed the previously hypothesised relationship that the fiery minivet and [[small minivet]] (''Pericrocotus cinnamomeus'') are [[sister species]].<ref name="Jonsson et al">{{cite journal|last1=Jønsson|first1=Knud A.|last2=Irestedt|first2=Martin|last3=Ericson|first3=Per G. P.|last4=Fjeldså|first4=Jon|title=A molecular phylogeny of minivets (Passeriformes: Campephagidae: ''Pericrocotus''): implications for biogeography and convergent plumage evolution|journal=Zoologica Scripta|date=2010|volume=39|issue=1|pages=1–8|doi=10.1111/j.1463-6409.2009.00401.x|url=http://www.nrm.se/download/18.396afe4c127aa7f284980005847/1367705202837/J%C3%83%C2%B6nsson%20et%20al%20Minivets.pdf|archive-date=2018-05-18|access-date=2018-05-18|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180518130246/http://www.nrm.se/download/18.396afe4c127aa7f284980005847/1367705202837/J%C3%83%C2%B6nsson%20et%20al%20Minivets.pdf}}</ref>

==Description==
The fiery minivet is {{convert|15|-|16.5|cm|abbr=on}} long. It is [[sexually dimorphic]].<ref name="Jonsson et al" /> The male has a glossy black head and mantle and an orange-red back. The wings are mostly glossy black, with orange-red edges to the [[covert feather|secondary coverts]] and an orange-red patch on the [[flight feather]]s. The gradated tail is black and orange-red. The chin and throat are glossy black, and the rest of the underparts is orange-red. The eyes are dark brown, and the beak and feet are black. The female has a grey head, with orange [[lore (anatomy)|lores]] and eye-rims. The back is lead grey, and the rump is orange-red. The wings are darker grey, and the wing-patch is paler than the male's. The underparts are yellow. The juvenile bird has sooty brown upperparts and sooty black flight feathers. The underparts are off-white from the chin to upper belly, the rest being pale yellow. After a post-juvenile moult, it becomes similar to the adult female.<ref name=Wells>{{cite book|last1=Wells|first1=David R.|title=The Birds of the Thai-Malay Peninsula|volume=2|date=2010|publisher=Bloomsbury|isbn=978-1-4081-3313-2|pages=99–100|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DgZCBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA99}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
This species is found in Tenasserim, the [[Thai-Malay Peninsula]], [[Sumatra]] and its satellite islands, [[Borneo]] and [[Palawan]].<ref name=Wells /> It is [[locally extinct]] in [[Singapore]].<ref name=Jeyarajasingam>{{cite book|last1=Jeyarajasingam|first1=Allen|title=A Field Guide to the Birds of Peninsular Malaysia and Singapore|date=2012|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-963943-4|page=272|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UIh8RwJpYYkC&pg=PA272}}</ref> It lives in the canopy of lowland [[broadleaf forest]], [[forest edge]]s, [[peat swamp forest]], well-grown [[secondary forest]], and coastal and [[mangrove]] forest.<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" /><ref name=Wells /> It is found up to {{convert|1200|m|abbr=on}} in elevation, mainly below {{convert|600|m|abbr=on}}.<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" /> It sometimes visits wooded gardens near forests.<ref name=Wells />

==Behaviour==
This minivet occurs in groups and also joins [[mixed-species foraging flock]]s. Its [[contact call]] is a ''swee-eet'' or ''twee-eet''.<ref name=Wells /> It catches insects in the air and from leaves and branches.<ref name=Jeyarajasingam /> Breeding has been observed from May to July. The shallow [[cup nest]] is built on a fork of a tree from twigs and fibres and camouflaged with lichen and pieces of bark. The eggs are pale yellowish, with brown and grey marks. [[Moulting]] has been recorded from June to September.<ref name=Wells />

==Status==
[[Deforestation]] due to [[logging]] and land conversion has likely caused the population to decline at a moderate speed. [[Forest fire]]s are also a threat, such as in 1997 and 1998.<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" /> The IUCN has assessed it as a [[near-threatened species]].<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" />

zh.extract:
火红山椒鸟（学名：Pericrocotus igneus）是山椒鸟科山椒鸟属的一种，分布于文莱、印度尼西亚、马来西亚、缅甸、菲律宾、新加坡和泰国。其自然栖息地为亚热带或热带的湿润低地森林以及亚热带或热带的湿润山地。该物种受栖息地减少威胁。


参考文献
BirdLife International. Pericrocotus igneus. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2009.   [2010-10-31] （英语）.
